title: Quantum Coordination without Conditioning under Restricted Information
abstract: We study coordination under restricted information, where classical local models fail to implement certain correlated distributions because agents cannot condition on past history. We show that quantum systems overcome this limitation even when using only separable states. Both classically diagonal encodings (shared latent variables) and separable states with noncommuting local structure (quantum discord) enable the implementation of joint distributions that are unattainable by any classical local rules under the same information constraints. The quantum advantage arises from enabling latent-variable coordination without requiring agents to condition on the latent variable itself -- a construction that succeeds where no classical local model can. Separable states with nonzero quantum discord provide an alternative mechanism for realizing such coordination. At the same time, quantum models remain strictly limited by the information structure: unlike perfect recall, they cannot reproduce fully adaptive dependence on realized past outcomes that are observationally indistinguishable. Thus, quantum correlations serve as a partial substitute for perfect recall.
summary: This paper demonstrates that quantum correlations can enable coordination in sequential tasks where classical agents fail due to an inability to condition on past history. It identifies two distinct quantum mechanisms—shared latent variables and quantum discord—that allow for the implementation of otherwise unattainable joint distributions. Ultimately, it positions quantum correlations as a partial substitute for the classical property of perfect recall.
main_problem: Investigating whether quantum correlations can implement joint distributions that are classically unattainable when agents are subject to information constraints like imperfect recall.
main_result: Quantum systems can act as a partial substitute for perfect recall, using both classical latent-variable encoding in separable states and non-commuting local structures (quantum discord) to achieve coordination impossible for classical local rules.
method: The study uses a probabilistic framework comparing classical local conditional rules against quantum measurements (POVMs) on shared states, applying extensions of Kuhn's theorem to imperfect recall settings.
model_or_system: A multi-stage sequential decision process involving agents (Alice, Bob, etc.) sharing multipartite quantum states, including both separable/diagonal states and discordant states.
key_observables: Target joint distributions (specifically anti-correlated distributions), quantum discord, and local measurement outcomes.
important_parameters: Information constraints (restricted access to past history $Y_k$), presence of quantum discord, and the degree of non-commutativity in local measurement bases.
main_assumptions: The model assumes no signaling between agents and that quantum correlations are fixed in advance and cannot reproduce fully adaptive dependence on indistinguishable past outcomes.
figures_summary: A tree representation of a binary sequential process where dashed lines indicate nodes that are indistinguishable due to information constraints.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of coordination under restricted information, defines the classical and quantum models, proves two theorems regarding diagonal and discordant mechanisms for coordination, and discusses the fundamental limitations of quantum models compared to perfect recall.
why_interesting: It provides fundamental insights into how quantum resources like discord can compensate for missing information in sequential processes, which is relevant to understanding the limits of quantum control and communication in networked systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Tree representation of the binary illustrative example. The dashed line indicates that the two second-step nodes are indistinguishable under the available information, so the local rule generating X2 cannot depend on the realized value of X1.
